Artificial intelligence is rapidly changing the nature of work across industries. From manufacturing floors to hospital operating theatres, from law offices to newsrooms, AI-powered tools are beginning to perform tasks that were once exclusively the domain of human beings. This transformation raises profound questions about employment, skill requirements, and the very definition of human labour.

The most immediate concern is job displacement. A report by McKinsey Global Institute estimates that up to 375 million workers worldwide may need to switch occupational categories by 2030 as automation takes over routine and repetitive tasks. Jobs in data entry, customer service, transportation, and basic legal and financial tasks are considered most at risk. At the same time, AI is creating new categories of jobs — data scientists, AI trainers, ethics reviewers, and more — though these typically require higher levels of education and technical skill.

Proponents of AI argue that, like the industrial revolution and the internet before it, AI will ultimately create more jobs than it destroys. History suggests that technology-driven disruptions, while painful in the short term, tend to result in long-term increases in productivity and living standards. Workers freed from repetitive tasks can focus on creative, collaborative, and empathetic work that machines cannot easily replicate.

However, the challenge lies in managing the transition. Governments and businesses must invest heavily in reskilling and upskilling programmes to prepare workers for the AI-driven economy. Education systems must also evolve, placing greater emphasis on critical thinking, creativity, digital literacy, and lifelong learning. Without such investment, the benefits of AI could be concentrated among a small elite, while large sections of the workforce are left behind.

Write your precis below (80-100 words):
questions.modelAnswer: Artificial intelligence is transforming work across all industries, raising concerns about job displacement. Up to 375 million workers may need to change occupations by 2030 as AI automates routine tasks. While AI creates new jobs in data science and AI development, these require higher technical skills. History suggests technology ultimately creates more jobs than it destroys. However, governments and businesses must invest in reskilling, education, and digital literacy to manage the transition fairly. Without targeted policy support, AI's benefits may concentrate among a few, deepening inequality rather than creating shared prosperity.

questions.text:
Read the following passage (approximately 300 words) carefully and write a precis in 80-100 words.

Water scarcity is one of the most critical challenges of the 21st century. While water covers about 71% of the Earth's surface, only 3% of it is freshwater, and less than 1% is easily accessible for human use. Rapid population growth, urbanisation, climate change, and inefficient water management have put immense pressure on this limited resource, threatening food security, public health, and economic development.

India is among the countries most vulnerable to water stress. The country is home to about 18% of the world's population but has access to only 4% of global freshwater resources. Over-extraction of groundwater for agriculture, industrial use, and domestic consumption has led to a significant decline in water tables across states like Punjab, Rajasthan, and Gujarat. Several Indian cities, including Chennai, Delhi, and Bangalore, could face severe water shortages in the coming decades.

Climate change is worsening the situation by altering rainfall patterns, accelerating glacier melting, and increasing the frequency of droughts and floods. The monsoon, India's primary source of freshwater replenishment, has become increasingly erratic, leading to both water shortages in some regions and devastating floods in others.

The solution lies in a multi-pronged approach. Water conservation through rainwater harvesting, watershed management, and drip irrigation must be scaled up. Water recycling and treatment of wastewater for reuse can supplement freshwater supplies. Smart water meters and better pricing mechanisms can discourage wasteful consumption.

At the policy level, there is an urgent need for integrated water management that coordinates across agriculture, industry, and urban planning. Public awareness campaigns can also play a role in promoting water-saving habits. Water is not a luxury — it is a fundamental human right.

Write your precis below (80-100 words):
questions.modelAnswer: Water scarcity is a major global challenge as only 1% of Earth's water is accessible for human use. India, with 18% of the world's population but only 4% of freshwater, is highly vulnerable. Excessive groundwater extraction and erratic monsoons due to climate change are worsening the crisis. Several Indian cities face severe future shortages. Solutions include rainwater harvesting, drip irrigation, wastewater recycling, and smart water metering. Integrated water management across agriculture, industry, and urban planning, along with public awareness, is essential to protect this vital resource for future generations.

questions.text:
Read the following passage (approximately 300 words) carefully and write a precis in 80-100 words.

India's startup ecosystem has emerged as one of the most vibrant in the world, ranking third globally in terms of unicorns — startups valued at over one billion US dollars. From fintech and edtech to agritech and healthtech, Indian startups are disrupting traditional industries and creating innovative solutions for both domestic and global markets. Cities like Bengaluru, Hyderabad, Delhi NCR, Mumbai, and Pune have become thriving startup hubs, attracting billions in venture capital annually.

The growth of the startup ecosystem has been driven by several factors. India's large young population provides both a huge consumer base and a talent pool of engineers and entrepreneurs. Rapid expansion of internet and smartphone penetration has created digital-first markets. Government initiatives like Startup India, launched in 2016, have provided regulatory support, tax benefits, and funding access. Incubators, accelerators, and angel investors have further supported entrepreneurship.

However, the ecosystem faces significant challenges. Access to risk capital remains uneven, with most investment concentrated in a handful of cities and sectors. Startups in tier-2 and tier-3 cities often struggle to find funding, mentorship, and market access. The sector has also seen several high-profile failures and governance issues in recent years.

Talent is another pressure point. While India produces a large number of engineering graduates, many startups report difficulty finding professionals with the right combination of technical skills, business acumen, and leadership qualities. Employee retention is also challenging, as competition for skilled talent is intense.

Despite these hurdles, India's startup story remains one of remarkable potential. With the right policy support, investment, and culture of innovation, India could become a global leader in startup-driven economic growth.

Write your precis below (80-100 words):
questions.modelAnswer: India's startup ecosystem ranks third globally, driven by a large young population, rising internet penetration, and government initiatives like Startup India. Cities like Bengaluru and Hyderabad have become major hubs attracting global venture capital. However, investment is concentrated in top cities, leaving tier-2 and tier-3 towns underserved. Governance issues and talent shortages remain key concerns. Though India produces many engineering graduates, startups struggle to find professionals with the right mix of technical and business skills. Despite these challenges, India's startup sector holds great potential to become a global driver of innovation and growth.

questions.text:
Read the following passage (approximately 300 words) carefully and write a precis in 80-100 words.

Education is one of the most powerful instruments for breaking the cycle of poverty. When children — especially girls — receive quality education, the benefits extend far beyond the individual. Educated individuals are more likely to find stable employment, earn higher incomes, and make better decisions about health, nutrition, and family planning. Communities with higher education levels tend to have lower infant mortality rates and more active civic participation.

Despite this, access to quality education remains deeply unequal across India. While enrolment rates at the primary level have improved significantly following the Right to Education Act of 2009, learning outcomes remain poor. A large proportion of Class 5 students cannot read a Class 2 level text or do basic arithmetic, according to the Annual Status of Education Report (ASER). The problem is worse in rural areas where schools often lack qualified teachers, functional toilets, libraries, or digital infrastructure.

Girls face additional barriers. In many parts of India, cultural norms, lack of safe transport, and early marriage continue to keep girls out of secondary and higher education. The economic value of a girl's unpaid domestic labour is often prioritised over her schooling, perpetuating intergenerational poverty.

Government schemes like Samagra Shiksha, PM eVidya, and the Midday Meal Scheme have made progress in increasing enrolment and reducing dropout rates. However, poor teaching quality remains the most critical unresolved issue. Teacher absenteeism, lack of training, and weak accountability undermine even well-funded programmes.

Technology offers promising solutions. Digital classrooms and AI-powered learning platforms can reach remote areas and improve learning at scale. India's NEP 2020 provides a forward-looking framework, but its success depends on strong implementation at the state level.

Write your precis below (80-100 words):
questions.modelAnswer: Education is a key tool for breaking poverty, improving employment, health, and civic participation. Despite rising enrolment in India following the Right to Education Act, learning outcomes remain poor, especially in rural areas with inadequate infrastructure. Girls face additional barriers such as cultural norms and early marriage. Government schemes like Samagra Shiksha and the Midday Meal Scheme have improved attendance but poor teaching quality remains a major concern. Technology and digital classrooms offer scalable solutions. The success of NEP 2020 depends on effective and consistent implementation across all states.

questions.text:
Read the following passage (approximately 300 words) carefully and write a precis in 80-100 words.

Urban India is at a crossroads. With over 500 million people living in cities and a number expected to reach 800 million by 2050, India faces an unprecedented urban challenge. Cities contribute over 60% of the country's GDP but rapid, unplanned urbanisation has brought traffic congestion, pollution, inadequate housing, and overstretched civic infrastructure.

The concept of smart cities has been presented as a solution. Under India's Smart Cities Mission, launched in 2015, 100 cities have been selected for integrated urban development using technology-driven solutions for water supply, waste management, traffic flow, and citizen services. The initiative has led to smart surveillance systems, integrated command centres, and digital grievance platforms.

However, critics argue that the Mission has focused more on infrastructure upgrades in select smart zones rather than addressing the needs of all residents, particularly the urban poor. Slum dwellers, migrant workers, and informal sector employees remain largely outside the reach of smart city benefits.

Affordable housing is another major concern. India faces a shortage of approximately 18.78 million urban housing units, concentrated among economically weaker sections. The PM Awas Yojana Urban has made progress, but construction of affordable homes at scale remains challenging.

Sustainable urban development requires thinking beyond technology. Inclusive planning that gives voice to all urban residents, investment in public transport, green spaces, and waste recycling, and stronger local governance mechanisms are essential components of making Indian cities truly liveable.

Write your precis below (80-100 words):
questions.modelAnswer: India faces a major urban challenge as its city population is set to reach 800 million by 2050, creating problems of congestion, pollution, and poor infrastructure. The Smart Cities Mission aims to address this through technology-driven development, but critics say it benefits only select zones and ignores the urban poor. Affordable housing remains a serious issue with a shortage of nearly 19 million units. True urban development needs inclusive planning, strong public transport, green spaces, and better local governance — not just technology — to make Indian cities liveable for all.

questions.text:
Read the following passage (approximately 300 words) carefully and write a precis in 80-100 words.

India has made considerable progress in health outcomes over the past few decades. Life expectancy has risen from around 50 years at independence to over 70 years today. Infant and child mortality rates have fallen sharply. Polio has been eradicated, and India is close to eliminating several other diseases including kala-azar and leprosy. The COVID-19 vaccination drive demonstrated India's capacity for large-scale public health mobilisation.

However, significant challenges remain. Communicable diseases such as tuberculosis, malaria, and dengue continue to pose a serious burden. Non-communicable diseases like diabetes, hypertension, and heart disease are rising rapidly, driven by changing diets, sedentary lifestyles, and urban air pollution. Mental health disorders are grossly underserved.

India's public health spending, at around 1.5% of GDP, remains well below the WHO recommended level of 5%. The result is a heavy reliance on out-of-pocket expenditure, which pushes millions into poverty each year. Government schemes like Ayushman Bharat PM-JAY have extended health insurance coverage to millions of poor families, but the quality and availability of healthcare in rural areas still falls short.

The relationship between good health and economic productivity is well established. Healthy workers are more productive and take fewer sick days. Healthy children learn better and contribute economically as adults. Poor health is both a cause and a consequence of poverty, creating a vicious cycle without targeted intervention.

Achieving truly universal health coverage requires sustained investment in public hospitals, primary health centres, health workers, and preventive care, alongside targeted programmes to address specific disease burdens and mental health gaps.

Write your precis below (80-100 words):
questions.modelAnswer: India has made health progress — rising life expectancy, falling child mortality, and a successful COVID-19 vaccination drive. However, communicable diseases and rising non-communicable diseases like diabetes and heart disease remain major concerns. India's public health spending at 1.5% of GDP is far below the WHO's recommended 5%, leading to high out-of-pocket costs that push many into poverty. While Ayushman Bharat has helped, rural healthcare quality remains poor. Universal health coverage needs sustained investment in hospitals, primary health centres, preventive care, and mental health services to break the cycle of poverty and illness.

questions.text:
Read the following passage (approximately 300 words) carefully and write a precis in 80-100 words.

Corruption remains one of the most serious obstacles to economic development and social justice. It distorts public resource allocation, undermines the rule of law, erodes trust in institutions, and disproportionately harms the poor who depend on public services. According to Transparency International's Corruption Perceptions Index, India has shown gradual improvement in recent years, though it still ranks among more corrupt nations.

Technology has emerged as one of the most effective tools in the fight against corruption. The Direct Benefit Transfer system, which channels government subsidies directly into beneficiaries' Aadhaar-linked bank accounts, has eliminated intermediaries prone to leakage. It is estimated that DBT has saved the government over Rs. 2 lakh crore by cutting out ghost beneficiaries.

The Right to Information Act of 2005 has also been a powerful weapon, empowering citizens to demand transparency and accountability from public authorities. Thousands of corruption cases have been exposed through RTI filings.

E-governance initiatives, online procurement systems, and automated regulatory approvals have reduced the discretionary power of bureaucrats, limiting bribery opportunities. The GST system, by replacing a complex web of indirect taxes with a digital self-policed regime, has also reduced corruption in taxation.

However, technology alone is not enough. Strong institutions, an independent judiciary, accountable political leadership, and a culture of zero tolerance for corruption are equally important. Whistleblower protection laws must be strengthened and anti-corruption agencies must be given greater independence and resources.

Write your precis below (80-100 words):
questions.modelAnswer: Corruption hampers development and harms the poor most severely. Technology has become a key anti-corruption tool: the Direct Benefit Transfer system has saved over Rs. 2 lakh crore by eliminating ghost beneficiaries, while the RTI Act has empowered citizens to demand accountability. E-governance and GST have reduced bureaucratic discretion and tax-related corruption. However, technology alone is insufficient. Strong institutions, an independent judiciary, political accountability, stronger whistleblower protection, and well-resourced anti-corruption agencies are equally essential to create a culture of integrity and transparent governance in India.

questions.text:
Read the following passage (approximately 300 words) carefully and write a precis in 80-100 words.

Women's empowerment is not just a moral imperative — it is an economic necessity. Research consistently shows that when women are educated, employed, and financially independent, families are healthier, children perform better in school, and communities are more prosperous. The World Bank estimates that gender gaps in labour force participation cost economies trillions of dollars annually in lost productivity.

India has seen significant progress in women's empowerment over recent decades. Female literacy rates have risen sharply. Women are increasingly visible in politics, business, judiciary, and the armed forces. Government schemes like Beti Bachao Beti Padhao, Sukanya Samriddhi Yojana, and PM Mudra Yojana have provided financial support and social recognition to girls and women.

Despite this progress, deep challenges remain. The female labour force participation rate in India is among the lowest in the world, at around 25%, compared to a global average of over 47%. Early marriage, domestic responsibilities, lack of safe transportation, workplace harassment, and social norms that restrict women's mobility continue to limit economic participation. The gender pay gap also persists across most sectors.

Violence against women remains a grave concern. India records a high number of crimes against women, including domestic violence, sexual assault, and dowry harassment. Legislative reforms have been made, but implementation on the ground remains inconsistent.

Financial inclusion of women is a particularly important lever for empowerment. When women have access to bank accounts, insurance, credit, and digital payments, they gain greater control over household finances. Jan Dhan Yojana has made progress here. Sustained effort across education, safety, employment, and finance is needed to achieve true gender equality.

Write your precis below (80-100 words):
questions.modelAnswer: Women's empowerment is both a moral and economic necessity. Educated and financially independent women raise healthier families and drive economic growth. India has made progress through rising female literacy and schemes like Beti Bachao Beti Padhao and PM Mudra Yojana. However, India's female labour participation remains low at 25%, held back by early marriage, safety concerns, and social norms. Violence against women remains serious despite legal reforms. Financial inclusion through Jan Dhan Yojana has helped, but sustained efforts across education, safety, employment, and access to credit are needed for true gender equality.

questions.text:
Write an essay in 250-300 words on the following topic:

'The Role of Microfinance in Poverty Alleviation in India'

Word Limit: 250-300 words
questions.modelAnswer:
THE ROLE OF MICROFINANCE IN POVERTY ALLEVIATION IN INDIA

Microfinance provides small loans, savings, and insurance to low-income individuals who cannot access traditional banking. In India, where many people remain outside the formal financial system, microfinance is a powerful tool for poverty alleviation.

The biggest impact of microfinance is economic empowerment. Small loans help poor individuals — especially rural women — start or expand small businesses. A woman who takes a Rs. 10,000 loan to buy a sewing machine can gradually build a tailoring business, earn a steady income, and repay the loan. Self-Help Groups (SHGs) linked to banks have created collective financial strength among rural women.

Microfinance institutions like Bandhan Bank and SEWA have reached millions of underserved borrowers. Beyond money, microfinance promotes financial literacy and savings habits. Women who access credit often become more confident and active in household decision-making.

However, microfinance is not without risks. High interest rates, aggressive recovery practices, and over-indebtedness have been reported in several states. The Andhra Pradesh microfinance crisis of 2010 highlighted the need for stronger regulation.

For microfinance to truly alleviate poverty, it must be accompanied by better regulation, client protection standards, financial education, and integration with government welfare programmes. When done responsibly, it can transform the lives of India's poorest communities.

questions.text:
Write an essay in 250-300 words on the following topic:

'Cyber Frauds: A Growing Threat to India's Banking Sector'

Word Limit: 250-300 words
questions.modelAnswer:
CYBER FRAUDS: A GROWING THREAT TO INDIA'S BANKING SECTOR

India's shift to digital banking has made banking faster and more accessible. But it has also opened the door to cybercrime that threatens customers and institutions alike.

Cyber fraud in banking takes many forms. Phishing tricks users into revealing passwords via fake emails. Vishing uses phone calls where fraudsters pretend to be bank officials. SIM swap fraud allows criminals to take control of a victim's mobile number. Social engineering manipulates victims into authorising fraudulent transfers. These attacks are growing more sophisticated and even careful users are falling prey.

RBI data shows financial fraud cases have risen sharply in recent years, with digital fraud accounting for the majority. Losses run into thousands of crores annually, and the psychological impact on victims is severe.

Banks have responded with multi-factor authentication, AI-powered fraud detection, transaction alerts, and dedicated helplines such as the National Cybercrime Helpline (1930). RBI has also issued cyber risk management guidelines for banks.

However, technology alone cannot solve the problem. Many frauds succeed not because of weak security but because victims willingly share OTPs and passwords. Banks must invest in sustained financial literacy campaigns, especially for elderly and rural customers who are most vulnerable.

As India moves toward a fully digital economy, cybersecurity must be a national priority. A safe banking system is not just a convenience — it is the foundation of public trust.

questions.text:
Write an essay in 250-300 words on the following topic:

'The Importance of Financial Literacy for Rural India'

Word Limit: 250-300 words
questions.modelAnswer:
THE IMPORTANCE OF FINANCIAL LITERACY FOR RURAL INDIA

Financial literacy means knowing how to make informed decisions about money. For rural India, where millions have limited access to formal banking, this knowledge is not just helpful — it is essential.

Rural households often manage finances through moneylenders, chit funds, and gold pawning. While familiar, these options often come with exploitative interest rates and no legal protection. A farmer borrowing from a moneylender at 36% interest instead of a Kisan Credit Card at 4% pays a devastating price simply for lack of information.

Financial literacy helps rural people access and use formal banking products — savings accounts, fixed deposits, insurance, and government schemes like PM Fasal Bima Yojana. Knowing how to use mobile banking or check Aadhaar-linked accounts builds real financial resilience for families.

For rural women, financial literacy is especially empowering. Women who understand savings, budgeting, and credit make better household decisions and protect themselves from financial dependence.

The government and RBI have launched financial literacy initiatives including camps, Business Correspondents, and RBI's Project Financial Literacy. NABARD and SIDBI also run targeted programmes for farmers and small entrepreneurs.

However, these programmes must reach far more people. Financial literacy education should be integrated into schools, community health centres, and agricultural extension services. When rural India becomes financially informed, it becomes truly empowered — and that is the real foundation of inclusive growth.

questions.text:
Write an essay in 250-300 words on the following topic:

'Non-Performing Assets (NPAs) in Indian Banks: Causes and Solutions'

Word Limit: 250-300 words
questions.modelAnswer:
NON-PERFORMING ASSETS IN INDIAN BANKS: CAUSES AND SOLUTIONS

Non-Performing Assets (NPAs) are loans that borrowers have failed to repay for more than 90 days. High NPAs reduce bank profitability, restrict lending, and can threaten the entire financial system.

India's NPA problem grew sharply after the economic boom of 2004-2011, when banks — especially public sector banks — gave aggressive loans to infrastructure, steel, and power companies that later became financially unviable. Political pressure sometimes influenced lending decisions. Global slowdowns and policy changes made conditions worse. Some large promoters chose not to repay despite being able to — called wilful default — further worsening the situation. Gross NPAs peaked at over 11% of total loans in 2018.

The government and RBI took several steps to address this. The Insolvency and Bankruptcy Code (IBC) of 2016 created a time-bound resolution process for stressed assets. The National Company Law Tribunal (NCLT) processed hundreds of cases. Bank recapitalisation injected capital into public sector banks to restore their strength. RBI's Asset Quality Review forced banks to recognise hidden NPAs.

As a result, gross NPAs have declined to around 5% in recent years — a significant improvement. However, sustained vigilance is essential. Better credit appraisal systems, independent bank boards, and strict action against wilful defaulters are necessary to prevent another NPA crisis.

A healthy banking system is fundamental to India's economic growth. Tackling NPAs effectively is not just a banking challenge — it is a national priority.

questions.text:
Write an essay in 250-300 words on the following topic:

'India's Demographic Dividend: Opportunity or Challenge?'

Word Limit: 250-300 words
questions.modelAnswer:
INDIA'S DEMOGRAPHIC DIVIDEND: OPPORTUNITY OR CHALLENGE?

India is the world's most populous nation with over 1.4 billion people, and also one of the youngest — with a median age of around 28 years. This large proportion of working-age youth is called India's demographic dividend — the economic growth potential that comes from a productive workforce relative to dependents.

The opportunity is immense. A young, large workforce can drive consumption, boost manufacturing, power services, and generate savings for investment. Many analysts compare India today to China in the 1980s and 1990s, when its young workforce fuelled decades of rapid growth.

However, a demographic dividend does not happen automatically. Young people need quality education and relevant skills. The economy must generate roughly 10-12 million new jobs every year just to absorb new workers. This is a massive challenge that India has not yet fully met.

Many Indian youth are unemployed or stuck in low-productivity informal jobs. India's female labour force participation rate of around 25% — among the lowest in the world — further limits the dividend's potential.

For India to truly benefit from its young population, it must invest heavily in education, healthcare, skill development, and employment generation. Entrepreneurship must be encouraged and barriers to formal employment reduced.

Without these investments, India's youthful population could become a burden rather than a blessing. The difference between a demographic dividend and a demographic disaster depends entirely on the policy choices made today.

questions.text:
Write an essay in 250-300 words on the following topic:

'The Impact of Social Media on Youth: Boon or Bane?'

Word Limit: 250-300 words
questions.modelAnswer:
THE IMPACT OF SOCIAL MEDIA ON YOUTH: BOON OR BANE?

Social media has transformed how young people communicate, learn, and see themselves. With billions using Instagram, YouTube, WhatsApp, and other platforms, social media is now a central part of youth life. Its impact, however, is deeply debated.

On the positive side, social media has made information and education accessible to all. Young people use YouTube and LinkedIn to learn new skills and build professional networks at no cost. Grassroots campaigns on climate, social justice, and public health have found powerful reach through digital platforms.

For young entrepreneurs and content creators, social media offers unmatched opportunity. A talented individual in a small town can build a global audience and earn an income entirely online. This democratisation of opportunity is genuinely empowering.

However, the harms are real. Research links heavy social media use among teenagers to anxiety, depression, low self-esteem, and loneliness. The pressure to present a perfect life online, the fear of missing out, and cyberbullying can severely damage mental health. Misinformation spreads rapidly, and young users are especially vulnerable.

Addiction is another concern. Apps are designed to maximise engagement through constant notifications, leading many young people to spend hours scrolling at the cost of sleep, study, and real-world relationships.

In conclusion, social media is neither wholly good nor wholly bad. Its impact depends on how it is used. Media literacy education, digital wellness habits, and stronger platform regulations are needed to ensure social media empowers rather than harms the next generation.

questions.text:
Write an essay in 250-300 words on the following topic:

'Sustainable Agriculture: Feeding India's Future'

Word Limit: 250-300 words
questions.modelAnswer:
SUSTAINABLE AGRICULTURE: FEEDING INDIA'S FUTURE

Agriculture employs nearly half of India's workforce and feeds 1.4 billion people. But India's current farming model — relying heavily on chemical fertilisers, water-intensive crops, and shrinking farmland — is increasingly unsustainable. Shifting to sustainable agriculture is no longer a choice; it is a necessity.

Sustainable agriculture means producing food in an environmentally responsible way without depleting natural resources. It includes organic farming, crop rotation, integrated pest management, agroforestry, and the use of drought-resistant seeds.

India has taken important steps in this direction. The Andhra Pradesh Community-Managed Natural Farming model has shown that farmers can lower input costs while maintaining or improving yields. NABARD funds organic farming clusters across the country. The Per Drop More Crop scheme promotes drip and sprinkler irrigation to reduce water wastage.

Technology is also helping. Satellite-based soil health monitoring, drone-based precision spraying, and AI-driven weather prediction tools are enabling farmers to make better decisions with fewer resources.

However, scaling these practices faces barriers. Many farmers fear yield losses during the transition from conventional to natural farming. Markets for organically grown produce are limited. Policy support — including better prices for sustainably grown food, easy credit, and training — is essential to encourage adoption.

India cannot choose between feeding people today and protecting its land for tomorrow. Sustainable agriculture is the path to achieving both — a future where farming is not only productive but also environmentally sound and resilient to climate change.

questions.text:
Write an essay in 250-300 words on the following topic:

'The Future of Work in the Age of Automation'

Word Limit: 250-300 words
questions.modelAnswer:
THE FUTURE OF WORK IN THE AGE OF AUTOMATION

Automation — powered by robotics and artificial intelligence — is changing what jobs exist, what skills they require, and how work gets done. For India, a country with a young and growing workforce, this transformation brings both opportunity and serious concern.

Automation threatens routine and repetitive jobs most — manufacturing, data entry, customer service, and basic financial or legal tasks. The ILO estimates that a large share of jobs in developing nations like India are at high risk in the coming decades.

However, history offers reassurance. Every industrial revolution from the steam engine to the internet ultimately created more jobs than it destroyed. Automation raises productivity, lowers costs, and creates demand for new products and services — generating new employment in the process.

The jobs of the future will require creativity, empathy, complex problem-solving, and interpersonal skills — qualities that are difficult to automate. New roles in AI ethics, data science, green energy, and digital healthcare are already emerging.

For India to benefit from this shift, it must urgently reform its education and training systems. Vocational training must align with actual industry needs. Social protection systems must support workers displaced during the transition period.

Automation is not the enemy of work — poor preparation is. With the right investment in human capital, digital infrastructure, and forward-looking policy, India can turn the age of automation into a great era of opportunity for its young workforce.

questions.text:
Write an essay in 250-300 words on the following topic:

'India's Infrastructure Development: Progress and Challenges'
questions.modelAnswer:
INDIA'S INFRASTRUCTURE DEVELOPMENT: PROGRESS AND CHALLENGES

Infrastructure — roads, railways, ports, airports, power grids, and digital networks — determines how efficiently people, goods, and ideas move across a country. In recent years, India has made impressive infrastructure strides, but significant challenges remain.

Progress has been remarkable in several areas. India now builds over 10,000 km of national highways annually. Vande Bharat trains run on dozens of routes. New airports have been built or expanded across the country. India's digital infrastructure — Jio's 4G revolution, widespread broadband, and UPI — is globally admired. The National Infrastructure Pipeline envisions Rs. 111 lakh crore in investment over five years.

However, serious challenges persist. Land acquisition disputes delay projects for years. Regulatory bottlenecks and inter-ministerial coordination failures slow execution. Many projects face cost overruns and timeline slippages. Rural areas still lag in last-mile road connectivity, drinking water supply, and reliable power.

Environmental concerns are also significant. Large infrastructure projects often conflict with forest conservation and community rights, requiring careful balance between development and sustainability.

For India to realise its infrastructure potential, it must improve regulatory efficiency, streamline land acquisition, and ensure that the benefits of infrastructure reach all regions — not just major cities. Infrastructure that does not reach the last mile is infrastructure that falls short of its promise.

India's infrastructure journey is firmly on an upward path. With strong execution and inclusive planning, it can become the backbone of the nation's next phase of economic growth.

questions.text:
Write an essay in 250-300 words on the following topic:

'India's G20 Presidency: Achievements and Global Significance'

Word Limit: 250-300 words
questions.modelAnswer:
INDIA'S G20 PRESIDENCY: ACHIEVEMENTS AND GLOBAL SIGNIFICANCE

India held the Presidency of the G20 from December 2022 to November 2023 — a historic moment reflecting its growing stature as a global power. Under the theme 'Vasudhaiva Kutumbakam' (One Earth, One Family, One Future), India championed the cause of the Global South and brought the concerns of developing nations to the world stage.

One of the most significant achievements was the inclusion of the African Union as a permanent G20 member, expanding representation of the developing world. India also secured consensus on the New Delhi Declaration, which addressed global economic challenges, debt restructuring for developing nations, climate finance, and sustainable development goals.

India placed the digital economy at the centre of its agenda, showcasing UPI, Aadhaar, and the India Stack as models of inclusive digital public infrastructure. The G20 endorsed a framework for Digital Public Infrastructure — largely inspired by India's experience — to help other countries build similar systems.

The Presidency also elevated India's soft power. Over 200 meetings were held across 60 Indian cities, showcasing India's cultural diversity, heritage, and economic dynamism. It was praised as one of the most inclusive G20 Presidencies in history.

However, India faced criticism for limited consensus on the Russia-Ukraine conflict, reflecting the challenges of bridging geopolitical divides.

Overall, India's G20 Presidency was a defining moment — affirming its role as a bridge between the developed and developing worlds and as a responsible stakeholder in global governance.

questions.text:
Read the passage carefully and answer the questions. Write 30-50 words per answer.

THE RESERVE BANK OF INDIA: GUARDIAN OF INDIA'S MONETARY SYSTEM

The Reserve Bank of India (RBI), established in 1935, is India's central bank and the apex regulatory authority for the country's financial and monetary system. It has evolved from a colonial institution into one of the most respected central banks in the developing world.

The RBI performs several critical functions. As the issuer of currency, it controls the money supply. It formulates monetary policy — primarily through setting the repo rate, which is the rate at which banks borrow short-term funds from the RBI. When the RBI raises the repo rate, borrowing becomes more expensive, helping control inflation. When it lowers the rate, credit becomes cheaper, stimulating growth.

The RBI also functions as the banker to the Government of India, managing government accounts and facilitating borrowing. It acts as the banker to banks, providing short-term liquidity and acting as the lender of last resort during financial stress.

Regulation and supervision of commercial banks, cooperative banks, NBFCs, and payment system operators fall under the RBI's purview. It sets prudential norms — such as capital adequacy ratios and liquidity requirements — to ensure the safety of the banking sector. When banks face difficulties, the RBI intervenes to protect depositors and maintain stability.

The RBI also manages India's foreign exchange reserves and implements the Foreign Exchange Management Act (FEMA). In recent years, the RBI has played a central role in developing India's digital payment infrastructure, overseeing the National Payments Corporation of India (NPCI) and authorising payment aggregators and wallets.

QUESTIONS:
(a) What is the repo rate and how does the RBI use it to manage inflation and growth?
(b) What is meant by the term 'lender of last resort'?
(c) How does the RBI help maintain the safety of deposits held in banks?
(d) What role does the RBI play in India's digital payment ecosystem?
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) The repo rate is the rate at which the RBI lends short-term funds to commercial banks. When raised, borrowing becomes costly, reducing spending and controlling inflation. When lowered, credit becomes cheaper, encouraging investment and stimulating economic growth.

(b) Lender of last resort means that when banks face a liquidity crisis and cannot borrow elsewhere, the RBI steps in with emergency funds to prevent the bank from collapsing, thereby protecting the broader financial system from failure.

(c) The RBI sets prudential norms such as capital adequacy ratios and liquidity requirements that all banks must maintain. It also supervises banks regularly and intervenes when a bank faces difficulties, preventing failures that could harm depositors and the financial system.

(d) The RBI oversees India's digital payment infrastructure by regulating the NPCI and authorising payment aggregators and digital wallets. It sets security standards and rules for digital transactions, ensuring a safe, efficient, and inclusive payment ecosystem across the country.

questions.text:
Read the passage carefully and answer the questions. Write 30-50 words per answer.

GST: INDIA'S BIGGEST TAX REFORM

The Goods and Services Tax (GST), implemented on 1st July 2017, is widely regarded as the biggest indirect tax reform in India since independence. It replaced a complex, multi-layered tax system — including Central Excise Duty, Service Tax, and VAT — with a unified tax structure under the motto 'One Nation, One Tax, One Market.'

GST is a destination-based tax, levied at the point of consumption rather than production. It follows a dual structure: the Centre levies CGST and states levy SGST on intra-state sales, while IGST is applied to inter-state transactions. Businesses must register, file returns, and pay taxes through the GSTN portal.

GST brought several benefits. It eliminated the cascading effect of taxes — where taxes were levied on taxes at multiple stages, making goods unnecessarily expensive. GST simplified compliance for businesses operating across multiple states. The input tax credit mechanism allows businesses to offset GST paid on inputs against GST collected on sales, preventing double taxation.

GST has also improved tax buoyancy. Monthly GST collections grew from around Rs. 90,000 crore when the tax was introduced to over Rs. 1.7 lakh crore per month in recent years, reflecting better compliance and economic growth.

However, GST has faced criticism. Small businesses find the compliance process complex and frequent online filings burdensome. Multiple tax rate slabs (5%, 12%, 18%, 28%) add complexity. Certain sectors — petroleum products and alcohol — remain outside the GST framework, limiting its uniformity.

QUESTIONS:
(a) What was the main problem with India's tax system before GST?
(b) Explain the concept of 'input tax credit' under GST.
(c) What does 'destination-based tax' mean in the context of GST?
(d) What are the main criticisms of GST despite its benefits?
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) Before GST, India had a complex multi-layered tax system with separate central and state taxes including Excise Duty, VAT, and Service Tax. This created a cascading effect where taxes were applied on taxes, making goods expensive and compliance very difficult for businesses operating across states.

(b) Input tax credit allows a business to reduce the GST it collects from customers by the amount of GST it already paid on its own purchases. This prevents double taxation and lowers the overall tax burden, making the system more efficient for businesses at every stage of production.

(c) A destination-based tax means the tax is collected in the state where goods or services are finally consumed, not where they are produced. This ensures that GST revenue flows to the consuming state, which is considered fairer and encourages states to support consumption and investment.

(d) GST is criticised for its complex compliance process, which burdens small businesses with frequent online filings. Multiple tax rate slabs (5%, 12%, 18%, 28%) add confusion. Petroleum products and alcohol still remaining outside GST limits its uniformity and reduces the reform's full potential impact.

questions.text:
Read the passage carefully and answer the questions. Write 30-50 words per answer.

CLIMATE CHANGE AND INDIA: IMPACT AND RESPONSE

Climate change refers to long-term shifts in global temperatures and weather patterns, largely caused by human activities such as burning fossil fuels, deforestation, and industrial processes. India is among the countries most vulnerable to its impacts, even though its per capita carbon emissions are far below the global average.

The impacts are already visible. Extreme weather events — droughts, floods, cyclones, and heatwaves — are becoming more frequent and severe. The Himalayan glaciers, which feed major rivers including the Ganga, Yamuna, and Brahmaputra, are melting at an accelerating rate, threatening the water security of hundreds of millions. Low-lying coastal areas like Mumbai and Chennai face the risk of sea-level rise. Agriculture is particularly vulnerable, as erratic monsoons and rising temperatures reduce crop yields and threaten food security.

Despite these challenges, India has taken an active role in global climate negotiations. At COP26 in Glasgow, India committed to achieving net zero carbon emissions by 2070, generating 50% of its energy from non-fossil fuel sources by 2030, and reducing the emissions intensity of its economy by 45% by 2030. India's renewable energy expansion has been impressive.

Domestically, India has launched green initiatives including the National Action Plan on Climate Change (NAPCC) and co-founded the International Solar Alliance with France to promote solar energy globally. Electric vehicle policies and green building codes are being implemented across states.

However, India faces a fundamental tension: lifting hundreds of millions out of poverty requires economic growth that currently depends largely on coal-based energy. Balancing development and climate action remains India's greatest challenge.

QUESTIONS:
(a) Why is India particularly vulnerable to climate change despite low per capita emissions?
(b) What risks do melting Himalayan glaciers pose for India?
(c) What targets did India set at the COP26 summit in Glasgow?
(d) Explain the fundamental tension India faces in addressing climate change.
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) India is vulnerable due to its geography — long coastlines, Himalayan glaciers, monsoon-dependent agriculture, and dense river systems. Extreme weather events like floods, droughts, and cyclones directly affect millions of poor communities who have very limited resources to adapt to or recover from climate shocks.

(b) Melting glaciers threaten water security for hundreds of millions since major rivers like the Ganga and Brahmaputra depend on them. In the short term, glacial melting causes floods; in the long term it leads to reduced river flow, causing severe water shortages for drinking, farming, and industry.

(c) At COP26, India committed to net zero carbon emissions by 2070, generating 50% of energy from non-fossil fuel sources by 2030, and reducing the emissions intensity of its economy by 45% from 2005 levels by 2030 — reflecting ambition while acknowledging India's ongoing development needs.

(d) India needs rapid economic growth to reduce poverty, which currently requires large amounts of energy — mostly from coal. Shifting to clean energy is necessary for climate goals but takes time and investment. Balancing urgent development needs with the equally urgent need to reduce emissions is India's core challenge.

questions.text:
Read the passage carefully and answer the questions. Write 30-50 words per answer.

INDIA'S BANKING SECTOR: EVOLUTION AND REFORM

The evolution of India's banking sector is closely linked to the country's economic history. Before independence, the sector was dominated by foreign banks primarily serving trade and the colonial administration. After independence, the government reshaped banking to serve the broader needs of the new nation.

The nationalisation of 14 major commercial banks in 1969 was a defining moment. It directed credit toward priority sectors like agriculture, small industry, and weaker sections of society. A second round of nationalisation in 1980 brought six more banks under government control, and branch networks expanded rapidly to rural and semi-urban areas.

The economic liberalisation of 1991 transformed banking again. New private sector banks were licensed, bringing modern technology, better customer service, and competitive pressure. Banks like HDFC, ICICI, and Axis gained market share with their technology-first approach, ATMs, and internet banking.

The 2000s saw the rise of digital banking. Internet banking, mobile banking, and UPI changed how Indians transact. Jan Dhan Yojana, launched in 2014, brought millions of unbanked households into the formal system using the JAM trinity — Jan Dhan accounts, Aadhaar, and Mobile phones.

India's banking sector today includes public sector banks, private banks, cooperative banks, small finance banks, payment banks, and NBFCs, each serving different population segments. RBI regulatory reforms — including stricter capital norms, digital lending guidelines, and consumer protection measures — continue to shape this central pillar of India's economy.

QUESTIONS:
(a) What was the purpose of bank nationalisation in 1969?
(b) How did liberalisation in 1991 change the banking sector?
(c) What is the JAM trinity and what problem does it aim to solve?
(d) How has digital banking changed the way Indians access financial services?
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) Bank nationalisation in 1969 aimed to bring banking services to the masses by directing credit to underserved sectors like agriculture, small industries, and weaker communities. It reduced the concentration of banking power in private hands and enabled the government to use banks as tools for economic development and social equity.

(b) Liberalisation in 1991 allowed new private banks to enter the market, introducing competition, modern technology, ATMs, and internet banking. Banks like HDFC and ICICI rapidly gained customers, forcing public sector banks to modernise their technology and improve customer service to remain competitive.

(c) JAM trinity refers to Jan Dhan Yojana accounts, Aadhaar identification, and Mobile phones. It solves the problem of financial exclusion by giving unbanked citizens a bank account linked to a unique digital identity accessible via mobile, enabling direct government benefit transfers to reach beneficiaries without leakage or intermediaries.

(d) Digital banking through internet banking, mobile apps, and UPI has made it possible to transfer money, pay bills, and access services anytime without visiting a branch. This has improved convenience dramatically, reduced transaction costs, and extended financial services to remote and rural populations previously excluded from formal banking.

questions.text:
Read the passage carefully and answer the questions. Write 30-50 words per answer.

INDIA'S SPACE PROGRAMME: FROM ARYABHATA TO CHANDRAYAAN

India's space journey began in 1963 with a sounding rocket launch from Thumba in Kerala. Six decades later, India is one of the world's leading spacefaring nations. The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO), established in 1969, has driven this remarkable journey.

ISRO's early satellites focused on practical applications — communication, weather forecasting, and remote sensing for agriculture and disaster management. The INSAT and IRS satellite series transformed how India managed resources across its vast geography.

India's commercial success became evident with the PSLV (Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle), now among the world's most reliable and affordable launch vehicles. In 2017, ISRO set a world record by launching 104 satellites in a single mission.

India's planetary missions have been equally impressive. Chandrayaan-1, launched in 2008, made the historic discovery of water molecules on the Moon. The Mars Orbiter Mission (Mangalyaan), launched in 2013, made India the first country to reach Mars on its first attempt. Chandrayaan-3, in August 2023, achieved a historic soft landing near the Moon's south pole — making India the first country to do so. This region is believed to contain water ice, crucial for future lunar habitation.

India is now developing reusable rocket technology, preparing for a crewed spaceflight programme (Gaganyaan), and opening its space infrastructure to private sector companies. This commercialisation is accelerating India's growth as a global space services provider.

QUESTIONS:
(a) Why is the PSLV considered one of India's most important space achievements?
(b) What was the significance of Chandrayaan-1's discovery?
(c) Why is the Moon's south pole important for future space exploration?
(d) What does commercialisation of India's space sector mean and why is it significant?
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) The PSLV is reliable, affordable, and technically precise, making India a competitive option for launching commercial satellites. Its success in launching 104 satellites in one mission demonstrated India's world-class capability and helped ISRO earn significant foreign revenue while reducing the cost of access to space globally.

(b) Chandrayaan-1's discovery of water molecules on the Moon in 2008 was a landmark finding. It showed the Moon is not completely dry, which has major implications for future lunar missions — water can support human habitation and be converted into rocket fuel for deep space exploration.

(c) The Moon's south pole is believed to contain water ice in permanently shadowed craters. This water is vital for future missions — it can be used for drinking, oxygen production, and manufacturing rocket fuel, making the south pole the most strategically important location for future human lunar bases.

(d) Commercialisation means allowing private companies to use ISRO's infrastructure and technology to build and launch satellites and develop space technology independently. This is significant because it creates a new industry, reduces financial burden on ISRO, generates jobs, and positions India as a major global space services provider.

questions.text:
Read the passage carefully and answer the questions. Write 30-50 words per answer.

INDIA'S HEALTH INSURANCE LANDSCAPE

Health insurance is a financial product that covers medical treatment costs in exchange for regular premium payments. In India, where public health spending is low and out-of-pocket medical expenses push millions into poverty, health insurance plays a critical role.

India's health insurance market has two broad segments: government schemes and private insurance. The government's flagship programme is Ayushman Bharat – Pradhan Mantri Jan Arogya Yojana (PM-JAY), launched in 2018. It is the world's largest government-funded health insurance programme, providing coverage of up to Rs. 5 lakh per family per year for hospitalisation to approximately 50 crore beneficiaries from the bottom 40% of India's income distribution. Beneficiaries can avail cashless treatment at any empanelled hospital.

On the private side, general insurance companies offer products ranging from basic hospitalisation to comprehensive policies covering maternity, outpatient care, and critical illness. Urban middle-class Indians are the primary private health insurance customers, and penetration grew significantly after the COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the financial risk of serious illness.

However, challenges remain. Insurance literacy is low, especially in rural areas. Mis-selling by agents and complex policies with hidden exclusions are common grievances. Claim rejection rates, particularly for pre-existing conditions, erode public trust.

The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI) has introduced stricter norms on policy language, claim settlement timelines, and standardised products. Universal health coverage — ensuring every Indian has quality healthcare without financial hardship — remains the ultimate goal.

QUESTIONS:
(a) How does PM-JAY help protect vulnerable families from financial hardship due to illness?
(b) Why has private health insurance grown significantly since the COVID-19 pandemic?
(c) What is 'insurance literacy' and why is it important?
(d) What steps has IRDAI taken to build trust in the health insurance sector?
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) PM-JAY provides free cashless hospitalisation up to Rs. 5 lakh per family per year for poor and vulnerable households. Families from the bottom 40% of earners can receive medical care at empanelled hospitals without any payment at the time of treatment, protecting them from financially devastating medical bills.

(b) The COVID-19 pandemic made millions of Indians personally experience severe illness and enormous hospital bills. This firsthand experience highlighted the financial risk of being uninsured and prompted many middle-class families to purchase health insurance for the first time, driving rapid growth in private coverage.

(c) Insurance literacy is the understanding of how insurance works, what it covers, and how to file claims successfully. It is important because without it, customers cannot make informed choices, may purchase unsuitable products, and may be unable to claim benefits they are entitled to when they most need them.

(d) IRDAI has introduced stricter norms requiring clearer and simpler policy language, standardised products, faster claim settlement timelines, and stronger regulations against mis-selling by agents. These measures aim to make the insurance process more transparent and ensure policyholders receive fair treatment when making claims.

questions.text:
Read the passage carefully and answer the questions. Write 30-50 words per answer.

THE CONCEPT OF INCLUSIVE GROWTH

Economic growth measures how fast an economy is expanding — usually expressed as a percentage increase in GDP. India has achieved impressive growth, becoming the world's fifth-largest economy. But growth figures alone do not tell the full story. The question that matters equally is: who is benefiting from growth?

Inclusive growth is a concept that goes beyond economic expansion to ask whether the benefits of growth are broadly shared — reaching the poor, the marginalised, women, rural communities, and other disadvantaged groups. An economy that grows rapidly but concentrates gains among a small elite while leaving large sections behind is not truly inclusive.

India's growth has been uneven. The IT and financial services sectors have created enormous wealth, primarily for educated, urban Indians. But farmers, construction workers, domestic workers, and informal sector employees — who form the majority of India's workforce — have seen far more modest gains. Inequality has increased in recent decades.

For growth to be truly inclusive, several conditions must be met. The benefits of growth must reach lower-income groups through rising wages, improved public services, and targeted welfare. Economic opportunities must be distributed across all regions, not just megacities. Social barriers based on gender, caste, religion, and disability must be addressed.

Government programmes like MGNREGS, PM Awas Yojana, Ujjwala Yojana, and the National Health Mission are designed to extend growth benefits to the most marginalised. Financial inclusion through Jan Dhan Yojana and digital payments has helped bring the poor into the formal economy. But much more remains to be done.

QUESTIONS:
(a) What is the difference between economic growth and inclusive growth?
(b) Why has India's growth been described as uneven?
(c) What conditions must be met for growth to be truly inclusive?
(d) Name two government programmes mentioned and explain how they promote inclusive growth.
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) Economic growth refers to the increase in a country's GDP — total value of goods and services produced. Inclusive growth goes further, ensuring the benefits of this expansion reach all sections of society including the poor, women, rural populations, and marginalised communities, not just wealthy or educated urban residents.

(b) India's growth has mainly benefited the IT and financial services sectors, creating wealth for educated, urban Indians. However, farmers, construction workers, and informal sector employees — who make up the majority of the workforce — have seen limited gains, and overall inequality measured by the Gini coefficient has increased.

(c) For growth to be inclusive, its benefits must reach lower-income groups through better wages, improved public services, and welfare programmes. Opportunities must spread across all regions including rural areas. Social barriers based on gender, caste, religion, and disability must also be removed so everyone can participate and benefit equally.

(d) MGNREGS guarantees 100 days of paid employment to rural households, providing income support during lean seasons and reducing rural poverty. Ujjwala Yojana provides free LPG connections to poor households, reducing dependence on polluting fuels and freeing women's time from collecting wood, improving health and enabling economic participation.

questions.text:
Read the passage carefully and answer the questions. Write 30-50 words per answer.

THE ROLE OF TECHNOLOGY IN MODERN BANKING

Technology has revolutionised banking over the past two decades, transforming it from a paper-based, branch-centric model into a digital-first ecosystem. Today, a customer in a remote village can open an account, apply for a loan, pay a bill, and invest in a mutual fund — all from a basic smartphone, without visiting a branch.

The core driver has been the rapid development of digital infrastructure. Mobile internet, affordable smartphones, and cloud computing have made digital banking scalable and accessible. In India, the Unified Payments Interface (UPI), managed by NPCI, has been the most transformative innovation. UPI allows instant, 24x7 money transfers between any two bank accounts using just a mobile number or QR code — free for individuals. In 2023, UPI processed over 100 billion transactions — more than any other real-time payment system in the world.

Artificial intelligence and machine learning are improving customer service, detecting fraud, assessing credit risk, and personalising financial products. AI-powered chatbots handle millions of queries without human intervention. Predictive algorithms assess a borrower's creditworthiness in seconds.

FinTech companies have disrupted traditional banking by offering innovative products in lending, payments, insurance, and wealth management — often with better experiences and lower costs. Banks are responding by partnering with FinTechs or building their own digital capabilities.

Cybersecurity remains the biggest challenge. As banking moves online, phishing, data breaches, and account takeovers are growing threats. Banks must continuously invest in biometrics, tokenisation, and encryption — and educate customers to stay safe.

QUESTIONS:
(a) What is UPI and why has it been described as transformative for India's financial system?
(b) How are AI and machine learning improving the loan approval process?
(c) What role are FinTech companies playing in the banking industry?
(d) What is the biggest challenge of digital banking and how are banks addressing it?
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) UPI is a real-time payment system enabling instant, free money transfers between any bank accounts using a mobile number or QR code, operating 24 hours daily. It has transformed India's financial system by making digital payments fast, accessible, and affordable for all income groups including small vendors and rural users.

(b) AI and machine learning analyse a borrower's transaction history and financial patterns to assess creditworthiness within seconds, replacing lengthy manual evaluation. This makes loan approvals faster and more accurate, reduces human bias, and enables banks to extend credit to customers who lack traditional credit histories or formal documentation.

(c) FinTech companies are disrupting traditional banking by offering digital financial products in lending, payments, insurance, and investment — with simpler interfaces and lower costs. They push banks to innovate and compete. Banks respond by partnering with FinTechs or developing their own digital platforms, ultimately benefiting consumers with better services.

(d) Cybersecurity is the biggest challenge as phishing, data breaches, and account takeovers grow rapidly. Banks address this through biometric authentication, tokenisation, end-to-end encryption, and AI-powered fraud detection systems. They also run customer awareness campaigns about safe online banking practices to reduce human error, which enables most successful fraud.

questions.text:
Read the passage carefully and answer the questions. Write 30-50 words per answer.

INDIA'S FOREIGN TRADE POLICY AND ITS IMPORTANCE

Foreign trade is the exchange of goods and services between countries. For India, international trade plays a vital role in driving economic growth, generating employment, and earning foreign exchange. India's trade policy aims to promote exports while managing imports to protect domestic industries and maintain a healthy balance of payments.

India's major exports include software and IT services, pharmaceuticals, textiles, engineering goods, gems and jewellery, and agricultural products. India is the world's largest exporter of generic medicines, supplying affordable drugs to over 200 countries. The IT sector generates billions in export earnings and employs millions of skilled workers.

On the import side, India's biggest items are crude oil and petroleum products, electronic goods, gold, and capital machinery. India imports about 85% of its crude oil, making it vulnerable to global oil price fluctuations. This is a major driver of India's trade deficit — the excess of imports over exports.

The government's Make in India and Aatmanirbhar Bharat initiatives aim to boost domestic manufacturing and reduce import dependence in sectors like electronics, defence equipment, and semiconductors. Production Linked Incentive (PLI) schemes have attracted significant investment in these areas.

India is also negotiating Free Trade Agreements (FTAs) with major partners including the UAE, Australia, and the European Union. FTAs reduce or eliminate tariffs on traded goods, making Indian exports more competitive abroad and giving Indian consumers access to cheaper imports. However, FTAs must be carefully designed to avoid harming domestic industries.

QUESTIONS:
(a) Why is India's dependence on crude oil imports considered a vulnerability?
(b) What is a Free Trade Agreement (FTA) and what are its benefits and risks for India?
(c) Why are India's pharmaceutical exports significant for the world?
(d) How do Make in India and PLI schemes aim to reduce India's trade deficit?
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) India imports about 85% of its crude oil. When global oil prices rise, India's import bill rises sharply, widening the trade deficit, weakening the rupee, and increasing domestic inflation. This dependence also makes India's economy highly sensitive to global geopolitical events that affect oil supply and prices.

(b) A Free Trade Agreement reduces or eliminates tariffs between member countries. Benefits for India include easier market access for exports and cheaper imports for consumers. The risk is that poorly negotiated FTAs could allow cheap foreign goods to flood India's market, damaging domestic manufacturers and threatening jobs in affected industries.

(c) India is the world's largest exporter of generic medicines, supplying affordable drugs to over 200 countries, especially developing nations. This makes Indian pharmaceutical exports critical for global healthcare access by providing life-saving medicines at prices developing countries can afford, while also being a major source of foreign exchange for India.

(d) Make in India and PLI schemes encourage domestic production of goods currently imported — such as electronics, semiconductors, and defence equipment. By boosting local manufacturing, India reduces its dependence on imports, which narrows the trade deficit, saves foreign exchange, creates jobs, and strengthens the domestic industrial base.

questions.text:
Read the passage carefully and answer the questions. Write 30-50 words per answer.

SKILL INDIA: BRIDGING THE EMPLOYABILITY GAP

India's workforce of over 500 million people is one of the country's greatest assets. But for this workforce to drive economic growth, individuals must have the right skills — not just educational qualifications. A 2023 survey found only about 45% of India's college graduates are directly employable in their field, reflecting a severe mismatch between what the education system produces and what employers need.

The government launched the Skill India Mission in 2015 through several flagship programmes. The Pradhan Mantri Kaushal Vikas Yojana (PMKVY) provides free short-term vocational training in over 300 job roles across sectors like construction, electronics, retail, beauty, and healthcare. Trainees receive a nationally recognised certificate. The National Apprenticeship Promotion Scheme (NAPS) encourages employers to hire and train apprentices. Industrial Training Institutes (ITIs) offer diploma-level technical training across the country.

The National Skill Development Corporation (NSDC), a public-private partnership, has been instrumental in bringing private training partners into the skilling ecosystem, broadening reach and improving quality through industry linkages.

Despite these efforts, challenges remain. Training quality varies across institutions. Dropout rates are high, particularly among women and rural youth. Placement outcomes after training are inconsistent. Recognition of vocational certificates by formal sector employers is limited compared to academic degrees.

India must also skill for the future. Automation and AI mean that skills in demand today may be obsolete tomorrow. Lifelong learning, digital literacy, and adaptability are the skills that will truly future-proof India's workforce. The challenge is building a skilling system that is both wide in reach and dynamic enough to keep pace with a rapidly changing economy.

QUESTIONS:
(a) What does the passage identify as the main problem with India's educated workforce?
(b) What is PMKVY and who does it target?
(c) What are the key challenges facing the Skill India Mission?
(d) Why does the passage emphasise the need for lifelong learning and digital literacy?
questions.modelAnswer:
(a) The passage identifies a severe skill mismatch as the main problem — only about 45% of Indian college graduates are directly employable in their field. This gap between what the education system produces and what employers actually need reduces India's ability to productively utilise its large and growing workforce for economic development.

(b) PMKVY provides free short-term vocational training in over 300 job roles across sectors like construction, healthcare, and retail. It targets unemployed and underemployed youth, giving them nationally recognised certificates after training to improve their employment prospects in both the formal economy and skilled informal sector jobs.

(c) Key challenges include uneven training quality across institutions, high dropout rates especially among women and rural youth, inconsistent job placement after training, and limited employer recognition of vocational certificates compared to academic degrees. These issues reduce the overall impact and attractiveness of vocational skilling programmes in India.

(d) Automation and AI are rapidly changing which skills are in demand, making current skills potentially obsolete soon. Workers who can continuously learn new skills and adapt digitally will remain relevant and productive. Without lifelong learning and digital literacy, large sections of the workforce risk unemployment as the economy evolves rapidly.

questions.text: You are an Assistant Manager at State Bank of India. A customer recently reported a fraudulent transaction of Rs 15,000 on their debit card, which occurred because they accidentally shared their OTP. Write a formal email informing the customer about the bank's investigation status, explaining the liability policy regarding OTP sharing, and outlining the next steps.
questions.modelAnswer:
To: ramesh.kumar@email.com
Subject: Investigation Update: Fraudulent Transaction on Debit Card

Dear Mr. Kumar,
This is with reference to your complaint regarding the unauthorized debit of Rs 15,000 from your account via your debit card.
Our cyber security cell immediately blocked your card to prevent further loss and has initiated a formal investigation. However, our preliminary review indicates that the transaction was validated using a One-Time Password (OTP) shared from your registered mobile number.
As per State Bank of India safety guidelines, sharing an OTP or PIN transfers the liability of the financial loss to the account holder, making a direct reversal difficult. Nevertheless, we have raised a dispute token with the merchant gateway to trace the funds.
We will update you on the final investigation report within seven working days. For your security, please never share banking credentials with anyone.
Thank you for your cooperation.

Yours faithfully,
Sneha Reddy
Assistant Manager
State Bank of India
Hyderabad Branch

questions.text: Write an email to the bank manager requesting to update your mobile number linked with your savings account, as your old number is no longer active.
questions.modelAnswer:
To: managerabcbank@gmail.com
Subject: Request to Update Registered Mobile Number

Dear Sir/Madam,
I hope you are doing well. I am writing to request an update to the mobile number linked to my savings account (Account No. 123456789012) held at your branch. My previously registered mobile number (9876543210) has been deactivated, and I would like to replace it with my new mobile number (9123456789) so that I can continue receiving transaction alerts, OTPs, and other important banking notifications.
Kindly let me know the procedure for updating my registered mobile number. I am willing to visit the branch and submit the required documents, including my Aadhaar Card, PAN Card, and a written application, as per the bank's guidelines.
I request you to process my request at the earliest to avoid any disruption in my banking services. I would appreciate your prompt assistance in this matter.
Thank you for your time and cooperation. I look forward to your confirmation.

Yours sincerely,
Karan Pawar

They understand that failure is a temporary setback rather than a permanent obstacle. By analyzing mistakes, adapting strategies, and maintaining discipline, individuals gradually develop the confidence and skills needed to achieve meaningful success in both personal and professional life.

Health is our greatest wealth. Eating fresh fruits and vegetables keeps us healthy. Regular exercise and proper sleep are also important. Drinking enough water every day helps the body stay active and fresh.

Every project we undertake is a complex puzzle that demands exceptional focus, from the initial brainstorming sessions down to the very final quality checks. We must avoid lazy habits and jump straight into solving tough challenges. By combining expert knowledge with a vibrant team spirit, we can easily conquer any budget goal or tight deadline.

Every single day, he weaves through thick, thorny bushes and climbs up jagged quartz cliffs just to glimpse the beautiful valley below. It requires a crazy amount of extra zeal and patience to explore such wild places, but he is completely unfazed by the risk. He simply packs his bags and quickly moves onward to the next big journey.

Jack quietly realized that big, heavy pumice stones were flying past his vexed face. He quickly jumped backward, avoiding the hazard just in time to save his prized possessions.
